tags.qa_note: Vessel is approximately 100m (320-327ft) long per DAN Alert Diver and Divertrek sources. Sunk July 25, 2003. Built 1954 in Naples, Italy as CS Salernum; renamed Charles L. Brown in 1984. Former AT&T cable-laying ship. Purchased by Statia for $1 USD. Lies on its STARBOARD SIDE at ~30m/100ft (not upright) — confirmed by DAN Alert Diver: 'The ship lies on its starboard side with an interesting debris field scattered in the sand below it.' Previous qa_note incorrectly implied upright orientation.
Anchor Point confirmed on leeward coast with 18th-century French anchor c.1750-1775 per Divertrek. Max depth approximately 60ft/18m.

Confirmed shallow shore dive ~55ft/17m max per Divertrek. Beads are Dutch/Amsterdam-made cobalt-blue pentagonal glass trade beads (NOT Venetian as previously stated — beads were manufactured in Amsterdam and used in the slave trade). Research: beads made in Amsterdam (lifeonthegoldenrock.wordpress.com), multiple bead origins confirmed by archaeology (Dutch 17th-18th c, African, Indonesian, Spanish, and some Venetian). Protected by STENAPA. Beads best found after storms disturb sand.

CORRECTED: Golden Rock Dive Center describes this as a modern wreck/artificial reef at 20m/66ft featuring a tugboat, barge, and hull. Not a natural shallow reef as previously listed (6m incorrect). Large schools of reef fish, angel fish, garden eels, Caribbean stingrays confirmed.
Golden Rock confirms: coral-encrusted lava flow, 10-15m/30-45ft range, maze of lava flows with overhangs. Type is wall/overhang on volcanic lava.
Shallow reef below historic fort with 17th-18th century artifacts (anchors, ballast stones, cannon). Protected by STENAPA.

Golden Rock confirms: archaeological site, 18m/60ft depth, beginner level, 2 min from shore. Remains of two 18th-century wooden merchant ships (hulls gone, anchors/ballast/cannon remain). Flying gurnards, stingrays confirmed by Divertrek.
Golden Rock confirms: coral-encrusted lava flow, 19m/63ft, beginner-intermediate. Nurse sharks under overhangs confirmed. Lettuce sea slugs in sandy areas noted by Golden Rock.
Five volcanic lava fingers extending from southern coast. Max 20m. Spur-and-groove formations with sandy channels. Intermediate due to southern exposure and potential current.
Terraced volcanic wall on northern leeward coast. Series of ledges 8-15m with overhangs colonized by cup corals and sponges.
